fact: December 15 sits exactly one week before the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, making it the perfect day to notice how short the daylight has become!
In Alaska, the sun rises after 10 AM and sets before 4 PM on this date - that's less than 6 hours of daylight to cram in a whole day!
Reindeer in the Arctic are now living in almost complete darkness, relying on their super-powered night vision to find food under the snow.
December 15 falls in the middle of Sagittarius season, when this archer constellation shoots across the night sky like a cosmic bow and arrow.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date brings summer heat while the North freezes - talk about opposite day on a global scale!
Evergreen trees are showing off right now in northern climates because they're the only plants still green and alive-looking while everything else looks brown.
The moon around this time often appears extra bright and crisp in the cold winter air because there's less water vapor to blur your view of space.
Squirrels are in full panic mode in northern regions, frantically digging up their buried nuts before the ground freezes solid.
In northern countries, the sun hangs so low in the sky that even at noon it looks like a permanent sunset with orange and pink colors.
December 15 marks when many bears have typically been snoozing in hibernation for about a month, and won't wake up until spring - the ultimate winter nap!
The air is so dry and cold in many northern places that your breath creates instant fog clouds, turning every kid into a mini dragon.
Owls are having the time of their lives because the long, dark nights give them extra hunting hours to swoop around like feathered ninjas.
